steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question tests Command of Evidence (Support). The team’s idea is that the shifting appearance of iridescent colors makes insects harder for other animals to see. The experiment compared how many iridescent wings versus solid-color (purple/blue) wings human participants could locate. The winning choice must show that participants found fewer iridescent wings, directly supporting the “harder to see” prediction.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — On average, participants found most of the purple wings and blue wings and far fewer of the iridescent wings.: Shows noniridescent wings were found more often, meaning iridescent wings were harder to spot — exactly what the team predicted. ✓
  • Choice B — On average, participants found the iridescent wings faster than they found the purple wings or blue wings.: Shows iridescent wings were easier (not harder) to locate, which directly undermines the team’s idea. ✗
  • Choice C — Some participants reported that the purple wings were easier to see than the blue wings.: Compares two noniridescent colors against each other; neither group is iridescent, so this finding is irrelevant to the team’s prediction about iridescence. ✗
  • Choice D — Some participants successfully found all of the wings on the leaves.: If all wings — iridescent and solid — could be found, that suggests iridescence provided no camouflage advantage, which if anything weakens the team’s idea. ✗
That matches Choice A — On average, participants found most of the purple wings and blue wings and far fewer of the iridescent wings..
